title: Listening Skill-Drill — Multiple Choice single (S3) — Choosing presentation software for a group project

topic_tags.S3: Two students discussing which presentation software to use for a group project (comparing four tools — Slidewright, Deckforge, Slidecraft, Prezzio — on cost, offline use, templates, collaboration, file size, rehearsal timer, accessibility and a backup plan)

sections.context: Two students, Aoife and Caedmon, discuss which presentation software to use for a shared group project. Aoife steers the choice while Caedmon raises and then drops objections; they weigh four named tools (Slidewright, Deckforge, Slidecraft, Prezzio) on cost, offline access, templates, real-time collaboration, file size, a rehearsal timer, accessibility for a colour-blind group member, and a backup format
sections.audio_intro: You will hear two students, Aoife and Caedmon, discussing which presentation software to use for their group project. First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 10. [pause:30s] Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 10.

audio_script.text: [emotion:friendly] Right, Caedmon, before we start building anything, we've gotta agree on what we're actually making the slides in. I keep flip-flopping between a few tools.

voice_settings._note: Aoife — keen IrlE student, drives the decision

audio_script.text: [emotion:thoughtful] Yeah, fair. So, um, my first thought was Slidewright — everyone's kinda used it before. But honestly… the price is the dealbreaker for me. It's gone subscription-only now.

[emotion:professional] See, that's exactly my worry too. We're a student group, we're not gonna pay a monthly fee. The thing that rules Slidewright out for me isn't the look of it — it's the [stress:cost] (Q1). Deckforge and Slidecraft both have a free student plan, so the money side basically decides it.

[emotion:doubtful] Okay, free it is, then. But here's what bugs me — half the time the library wifi just dies on you. If the tool only works in the browser, we're stuck. Can any of these actually run, you know, offline?

[emotion:friendly] Good shout. So I checked — Slidecraft is cloud-only, no offline mode at all. Deckforge, though, has a desktop app you can use with no internet. So the one that works [stress:offline] (Q2) is Deckforge — that pretty much settles it for me, given the dodgy wifi.
[emotion:thoughtful] Deckforge, grand. Although — and don't laugh — I'm rubbish at design. Last time my slides looked like… well, like a ransom note. Does it come with, um, ready-made layouts?

[emotion:enthusiastic] That's the best bit, actually. People assume the free version is stripped back, but Deckforge gives you the full set of [stress:templates] (Q3) even on the free plan. So you don't need to design from a blank page — the layouts are all there.

[emotion:thoughtful] [hesitate] Oh, that's a relief. Right, next thing — there's four of us in this group. Can we all be in the same file at once, or do we have to, like, take turns and email it round?

[emotion:professional] No, that's the whole point of it. The reason I'm pushing Deckforge over the others is the [stress:real-time collaboration] (Q4) — all four of us editing the same deck live, no emailing versions back and forth. That's the feature that actually matters for a group of four.

[emotion:doubtful] Now you're talking. Hang on, though — I tried something similar last year and the final file was massive, I mean it wouldn't even attach to an email. Is the export gonna be a, er, huge file?

[emotion:friendly] Funny you say that — I worried about the same thing. But Deckforge lets you export a compressed version, so the file stays [stress:small] (Q5). It squashes the images right down. So no, it won't be the giant file you're imagining.
[emotion:friendly] Brilliant. Okay, I'm sold on the tool. So what do we actually need to sort out before the presentation day itself?

[emotion:professional] Well, the marking rubric is strict on timing — eight minutes, no more. The thing I really want us to use is the built-in [stress:rehearsal timer] (Q6) — it records how long you spend on each slide so we can practise hitting eight minutes. Not the slide counter, the timer specifically.
[emotion:thoughtful] Makes sense, we always overrun. Um — one more. Priya's colour-blind, isn't she? I don't wanna pick colours she can't tell apart and have it look bad on the day.

[emotion:enthusiastic] I'm so glad you flagged that. Deckforge has a [stress:colour-blind check] (Q7) — you run it and it warns you if two shades clash for someone who can't distinguish them. We'll run that on every slide before we submit.
[emotion:thoughtful] Love that. Right, who's doing what? I'm happy to, er, build the actual slides if you handle the writing.
[emotion:friendly] Deal. So you take the [stress:slide design] (Q8) — the layout and the visuals — since you're the one fretting about how it looks, you'll care most about getting it right.
[emotion:thoughtful] Slides are mine, then. And you'll write the, um, actual content — the script and the notes under each slide?

[emotion:professional] Yeah, I'll take the [stress:written content] (Q9) — the words and the speaker notes. That leaves the practising. We should both rehearse, obviously, but someone needs to own the run-through and time us.

[emotion:thoughtful] Hmm, and honestly that should be Priya, shouldn't it? She's the calmest of us under pressure, so she's the best one to run the [stress:timed rehearsal] (Q10) and keep us to eight minutes. We design and write; she drills us.
[emotion:enthusiastic] Perfect — that's the lot sorted, then. Deckforge it is. Funny how I came in not knowing which to use and now it's all decided.

question_blocks.rubric: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.stem: Why do the students decide against using Slidewright?
Which tool can be used without an internet connection?
What does the free version of Deckforge surprisingly include?
Why does Aoife most prefer Deckforge for their group?

options.text: Everyone can edit the file together.
It is the cheapest of all.

questions.stem: What does Aoife say reassures Caedmon about the exported file?

answers.notes: Affirmative cue: 'The thing that rules Slidewright out for me isn't the look of it — it's the cost ... It's gone subscription-only now' = it now charges a fee (B). A (design looks dated) = Opposite-Meaning / mis-direction: Aoife explicitly disowns 'the look of it', so the look is named-then-rejected as the reason. C (nobody has used it) is the polar opposite of what Caedmon says ('everyone's kinda used it before') = Opposite-Meaning. Two distractors, two archetypes.

Affirmative cue: 'Deckforge, though, has a desktop app you can use with no internet ... the one that works offline is Deckforge' = A. B (Slidecraft) is named-then-rejected ('Slidecraft is cloud-only, no offline mode at all') = Outdated/Rejected. C (Prezzio) is plausible-but-unsaid — Prezzio is never linked to offline use, so picking it is an over-reach = Irrelevant Detail. Two distractors, two archetypes.

Affirmative cue: 'Deckforge gives you the full set of templates even on the free plan ... the layouts are all there' = C. A (extra cloud storage) is plausible-but-unsaid, a generic free-plan perk never mentioned = Irrelevant Detail. B (unlimited slides) is also plausible-but-unsaid, an Overgeneralised 'free = unlimited' leap. Two distractors, two archetypes.

Affirmative cue: 'The reason I'm pushing Deckforge over the others is the real-time collaboration — all four of us editing the same deck live'; the option recasts this as 'Everyone can edit the file together' (A) — a Tier-1 paraphrase, no keyword 'collaboration' reused. B (cheapest of all) = Partial Truth: it is free, but cost was the Q1 issue, not why she most prefers it here. C (nicest animations) is plausible-but-unsaid = Irrelevant Detail. Two distractors, two archetypes.

Affirmative cue: 'Deckforge lets you export a compressed version, so the file stays small ... it won't be the giant file you're imagining' = B. A (opens on any device) is plausible-but-unsaid = Irrelevant Detail. C (saves automatically) is plausible-but-unsaid, a generic cloud feature never mentioned = Irrelevant Detail. Caedmon's worry was specifically a 'huge file', so B answers it directly. Two distractors, two archetypes.

question_blocks.rubric: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.stem: Which feature does Aoife want the group to use for timing?
How will they make the slides work for Priya?
What job will Caedmon take on for the project?
Which part of the project will Aoife be responsible for?
Who do they agree should run the timed rehearsal?

answers.notes: Affirmative cue: 'The thing I really want us to use is the built-in rehearsal timer ... Not the slide counter, the timer specifically' = C. A (slide counter) is named-then-rejected in the same turn = Outdated/Rejected. B (auto-advance setting) is plausible-but-unsaid = Irrelevant Detail. Two distractors, two archetypes.

Affirmative cue: 'Deckforge has a colour-blind check ... it warns you if two shades clash ... We'll run that on every slide' = A. B (larger text) is plausible-but-unsaid: it addresses a different accessibility need (legibility), not colour, so it is Partial Truth / Irrelevant to a colour-blind member. C (audio version) is plausible-but-unsaid = Irrelevant Detail. Two distractors, two archetypes.

Affirmative cue: 'So you take the slide design — the layout and the visuals' (Aoife to Caedmon), confirmed 'Slides are mine, then' = B. A (writing the speaker notes) is the job Aoife takes (her job, attributed to wrong person) = Irrelevant Detail / mis-attribution. C (running the practice session) is Priya's job = Irrelevant Detail / mis-attribution. Two distractors, two archetypes.

Affirmative cue: 'I'll take the written content — the words and the speaker notes' = B ('Writing the words and notes'). A (choosing the colour scheme) belongs to the design job, which is Caedmon's, not Aoife's = Irrelevant Detail / mis-attribution. C (timing the run-through) is Priya's job = Irrelevant Detail / mis-attribution. Two distractors, two archetypes.

Affirmative cue: 'that should be Priya ... she's the best one to run the timed rehearsal ... We design and write; she drills us' = C. A (Caedmon) and B (Aoife) each take other jobs (design / written content); both 'should both rehearse' but neither owns the run-through = Partial Truth (they do rehearse, but do not run it). Two distractors, one archetype family (mis-attribution / partial truth).
